tier_1_fundamentals.title: Hypertrophy Fundamentals
tier_1_fundamentals.description: Core mechanisms of muscle growth. Passing this proves you understand how stimulating growth actually works at a basic level.

questions.question: Which of the following is the PRIMARY driver of muscle hypertrophy?
questions.explanation: Mechanical Tension — the force generated by cross-bridge cycling under load — is the dominant growth stimulus (Schoenfeld 2010). Damage and metabolic stress are byproducts, not independent drivers.

options.text: Muscle Damage (Soreness / DOMS)
Mechanical Tension
Metabolic Stress (The Pump)
Time Under Tension regardless of load

questions.question: According to the literature, which rep range can build muscle?
questions.explanation: Hypertrophy is similar across 5-30+ reps IF sets are taken close to failure (Schoenfeld et al. 2017). The key variable is proximity to failure, not the rep number itself.

options.text: Only 8-12 reps (the 'hypertrophy zone')
Only 1-5 reps (strength range)
Any range from ~5-30 reps, provided sets approach failure
Only 15+ reps (endurance range)

questions.question: What is Progressive Overload?
questions.explanation: Systematically increasing the training stimulus (load, reps, or sets) over time to force continued adaptation. Without it, the body has no reason to add tissue.

options.text: Changing exercises every week to 'confuse' the muscle
Repeating the same workout until it becomes easy
Systematically increasing tension or volume over time
Decreasing rest times between sets

questions.question: According to Morton et al. (2018), the optimal daily protein intake for hypertrophy is approximately:
questions.explanation: The largest meta-analysis on protein and muscle gain found that benefits plateau around 1.6 g/kg/day. The range 1.6-2.2 g/kg captures the confidence interval. More than 2.2 g/kg shows negligible additional benefit.

options.text: 0.8 g per kg of bodyweight (the RDA)
1.6 to 2.2 g per kg of bodyweight
4.0+ g per kg of bodyweight
Protein amount does not matter if calories are sufficient

questions.question: Why is sleep critical for hypertrophy?
questions.explanation: Pulsatile GH release peaks during deep sleep (SWS), and neural recovery occurs during REM. Chronic restriction below 7 hours impairs anabolic signaling and next-day training quality (Knowles et al. 2018).

options.text: It burns fat while you sleep
Growth hormone release and neural recovery peak during sleep
It is not important — you can compensate with more protein
It clears lactic acid from muscle tissue

questions.question: What training frequency per muscle group is supported by meta-analysis (Schoenfeld et al. 2016)?
questions.explanation: Training each muscle 2x/week is superior to 1x/week for hypertrophy. The advantage of 3x+ over 2x is small or non-existent for most trainees.

options.text: Once per week (classic bro split)
At least twice per week
Every single day for maximum MPS spikes
Once every 10-14 days

questions.question: True or False: Muscle soreness (DOMS) is a reliable indicator of a productive workout.
questions.explanation: Damas et al. (2015) showed muscle damage (and DOMS) drops to near-zero after the first few sessions of a movement, while hypertrophy continues for months. Soreness indicates novelty, not growth signal quality.

options.text: True — more soreness means more growth
False — DOMS reflects novelty and damage, not hypertrophic stimulus

questions.question: What defines a 'compound' exercise?
questions.explanation: Compound movements involve multiple joints (e.g., squat = hip + knee + ankle), recruiting more total muscle mass and allowing heavier absolute loads.

options.text: Any exercise performed with a barbell
Movement occurring across multiple joints simultaneously
Movement across a single joint only
Any exercise that feels difficult

questions.question: What is RIR (Reps In Reserve)?
questions.explanation: RIR estimates how many more reps you could have performed before muscular failure. 0 RIR = failure; 2 RIR = two more reps possible. Validated as a practical autoregulation tool (Helms et al. 2016).

options.text: Reps performed during the warmup
How many reps you have left before failure
Rest intervals recorded between sets
Reps done with degraded form

questions.question: To build muscle, most people need to be in a caloric state of:
questions.explanation: Hypertrophy requires energy and raw materials for tissue construction. A moderate surplus of ~200-500 kcal above maintenance is optimal. Larger surpluses add fat, not extra muscle. Beginners/detrained individuals can gain muscle at maintenance or slight deficit.

options.text: A large caloric deficit (the more the better)
Exact maintenance calories — no more, no less
A moderate caloric surplus (200-500 kcal above maintenance)
Caloric intake is irrelevant to hypertrophy

tier_2_intermediate.title: Intermediate: Fatigue Management & Stimulus Quality
tier_2_intermediate.description: Effective reps, stimulus-to-fatigue ratios, volume dose-response, and exercise biomechanics.

questions.question: What are 'Effective Reps' (or 'Stimulating Reps')?
questions.explanation: Only the last ~5 reps before failure fully recruit high-threshold motor units under sufficient tension. Earlier reps at low effort levels do not contribute meaningfully to hypertrophy.

options.text: The first 5 reps of every set (when you are freshest)
Reps performed with light weight for warmup
The reps near failure that recruit all available motor units
Any rep performed with strict form

questions.question: What is the 'Stimulus-to-Fatigue Ratio' (SFR)?
questions.explanation: SFR compares how much hypertrophic signal an exercise produces vs how much recovery cost it imposes. Machines and isolations typically have high SFR; heavy free-weight compounds have lower SFR due to systemic/CNS cost.

options.text: A measure of how exhausting a workout feels
Growth signal produced relative to systemic recovery cost
The ratio of sets to reps in a program
A metric only applicable to cardiovascular exercise

questions.question: Why should you try to accelerate the weight during the concentric (lifting) phase of each rep?
questions.explanation: Maximal concentric intent recruits the most motor units (Behm & Sale 1993). The nervous system responds to the INTENT to produce force, not the actual bar speed. Heavy loads or fatigue will slow the bar involuntarily — that combination of maximal effort + involuntary slow movement is where peak mechanical tension occurs. Deliberately slowing the concentric REDUCES motor unit recruitment and thus reduces the hypertrophic stimulus.

options.text: Faster reps burn more calories
Maximal concentric intent recruits the most motor units, maximizing mechanical tension
Slow concentrics are actually better for muscle growth
Speed does not matter — only the weight matters

questions.question: What is Junk Volume?
questions.explanation: Sets that exceed your MRV or are performed at too low intensity (high RIR) generate fatigue without proportional growth stimulus. They 'fill the bucket' of recovery capacity without adding to the muscle growth signal.

options.text: Training with very heavy weights
Any training volume — all volume is productive
Sets that add fatigue without meaningful hypertrophic stimulus
Light warmup sets before working sets

questions.question: According to Krieger (2010), what is the approximate volume dose-response per exercise per session?
questions.explanation: Krieger's meta-analysis found that 2-3 sets per exercise captures the vast majority of hypertrophic stimulus. Adding a 4th-5th set provides diminishing returns. The dose-response is logarithmic, not linear.

options.text: 1 set is always enough if taken to failure
2-3 sets captures most of the stimulus; beyond 4-5 sets, returns diminish sharply
More sets are always better — 8-10 sets per exercise per session is optimal
Volume does not matter, only intensity

questions.question: What is MRV (Maximum Recoverable Volume)?
questions.explanation: The highest training volume from which you can still recover. Exceeding MRV causes regression and overreaching. MRV is NOT fixed — it fluctuates with sleep, nutrition, stress, and training age.

options.text: The maximum weight you can lift once (1RM)
The volume ceiling beyond which performance regresses
A fixed number (20 sets per week) for everyone
The maximum number of reps per set

questions.question: Regarding exercise stability: why might a chest-supported row produce MORE hypertrophy per unit of fatigue than a barbell bent-over row?
questions.explanation: External stability removes balance demands from the CNS, allowing 100% of neural drive to target the prime movers. The barbell row also loads the spinal erectors isometrically, consuming systemic recovery capacity without additional back stimulus.

options.text: Free weights are always superior for muscle growth
High external stability directs all neural drive to prime movers, reducing systemic fatigue
Machines use lighter weight, so there is less risk
There is no difference between supported and unsupported movements

questions.question: Why is standardizing exercise form critical for tracking progress?
questions.explanation: If ROM shortens, momentum increases, or technique degrades, load increases become false indicators of strength gain. You are not getting stronger — you are getting sloppier. True progressive overload requires identical execution from session to session.

options.text: It looks more professional on video
To ensure load increases represent genuine strength adaptation
It reduces sweating during the workout
Form does not matter as long as weight goes up

questions.question: What is 'Active Insufficiency' and why does it affect exercise selection?
questions.explanation: A biarticular muscle cannot produce maximal force when shortened across both joints simultaneously. Example: lying leg curl (hips extended) puts hamstrings in active insufficiency. Seated leg curl (hips flexed) avoids this, producing better hamstring stimulus.

options.text: Reduced force production when a muscle is fully shortened at all joints it crosses
Being too tired to train effectively
A muscle being too weak for the selected weight
A phenomenon that only occurs in elderly populations

questions.question: Creatine monohydrate works by:
questions.explanation: Creatine saturates intramuscular phosphocreatine stores, improving ATP resynthesis during high-intensity muscle contractions. It is the most evidence-backed legal ergogenic supplement, producing ~5-8% strength improvements.

options.text: Increasing testosterone levels
Improving ATP regeneration for high-intensity contractions
Burning fat through thermogenesis
Replacing the need for dietary protein

tier_3_advanced.title: Advanced: Periodization, Biomechanics & Nuance
tier_3_advanced.description: Mesocycles, autoregulation, Banister model, and the subtleties that separate intermediate from advanced program design.

questions.question: In the Banister Fitness-Fatigue model, performance at any point equals:
questions.explanation: Calvert (1976) / Banister: Performance = Fitness - Fatigue. Both decay exponentially but at different rates (fitness slowly, fatigue quickly). A deload works by letting fatigue decay faster than fitness.

options.text: Volume x Intensity
Fitness minus Fatigue
Genetics plus Training Age
Protein intake times Sleep quality

questions.question: What is the purpose of a 'Resensitization' or maintenance phase?
questions.explanation: After sustained high-volume training, mTOR pathway sensitivity decreases. A strategic period at MEV (minimum volume) restores anabolic signaling sensitivity, making the next accumulation block more productive per set.

options.text: To completely lose all fitness and start over
To restore the muscle's sensitivity to training-induced growth signals
To practice new exercises for variety
There is no benefit — always train at maximum volume

questions.question: How should advanced trainees apply autoregulation?
questions.explanation: Adjusting session volume/intensity based on daily biofeedback (HRV, soreness, sleep quality, readiness). If readiness is low, reducing volume by 20-30% preserves the training stimulus while avoiding overreaching. Rigid adherence to a written plan ignores recovery variability.

options.text: Follow the program exactly, regardless of how you feel
Adjust volume and intensity based on daily recovery indicators
Train only when fully motivated — skip days when tired
Randomly vary everything each session

questions.question: What is 'Phase Potentiation' in periodization?
questions.explanation: Structuring sequential training phases so each phase builds the capacity needed for the next. E.g., a work capacity block prepares you to tolerate the volume of an accumulation block, which builds the muscle to express during a strength block.

options.text: Taking multiple supplements in sequence
Using one training phase to enhance the results of the subsequent phase
Performing all exercises as fast as possible
Doing cardio before resistance training

questions.question: Per Lyle McDonald's models, approximately how much muscle can a natural male intermediate (year 2-3) expect to gain per year?
questions.explanation: McDonald's model: ~1-1.25 kg/month year 1, ~0.5-0.75 kg/month year 2, ~0.25-0.5 kg/month year 3, and ~0.1-0.25 kg/month year 4+. An intermediate (year 2-3) can expect roughly 3-6 kg per year under optimal conditions.

options.text: 15-20 kg per year
3-6 kg per year under optimal conditions
There is no limit — gains are purely effort-dependent
0 kg — natural lifters cannot gain muscle after year 1

questions.question: What is the 'Repeated Bout Effect' and why is it relevant to program design?
questions.explanation: After initial exposure to an exercise, muscles develop protective adaptations that reduce damage from the same stimulus (Nosaka 2008). This is DESIRABLE — it means recovery becomes faster. Constantly rotating exercises to 'confuse' the muscle resets this protection and wastes recovery capacity on damage rather than growth.

options.text: Muscles grow resistant to damage from familiar stimuli — this is a good thing
Muscles stop responding to familiar exercises — you must constantly change
Repeated training causes overuse injuries in all cases
It only applies to endurance training, not hypertrophy

questions.question: What is 'Regional Hypertrophy' and why does it matter?
questions.explanation: Different exercises can bias growth in specific regions of the same muscle due to differences in fiber architecture, moment arms, and active insufficiency. The triceps long head produces more force in overhead extensions than pushdowns because the shoulder-flexed position avoids active insufficiency. This is about FORCE PRODUCTION at different joint angles, not about stretching. Program design should include exercises that allow each region of a muscle to produce maximal force.

options.text: It is a myth — muscles grow uniformly regardless of exercise
Non-uniform growth within a muscle depending on which region receives the most mechanical tension
Only growing one side of the body
Spot reduction of fat in specific areas

questions.question: How do Myo-Reps (or Rest-Pause sets) achieve efficient hypertrophy?
questions.explanation: By taking an initial activation set near failure, then performing clusters of 3-5 reps with 10-15 second rests, you keep the muscle in a fatigued state where every rep is an 'effective rep'. This bypasses the non-stimulating early reps of a conventional set, accumulating effective reps faster.

options.text: They involve very long rest periods between sets
They accumulate effective reps near failure with minimal rest, maximizing stimulus per unit of time
They use only cardiovascular exercises
They require extremely light weights only

questions.question: Regarding systemic vs local fatigue: which statement is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Compound free-weight lifts (deadlifts, squats) impose massive CNS/systemic fatigue that limits all subsequent training. Machine isolations impose predominantly local muscular fatigue, leaving systemic recovery capacity intact for other work.

options.text: All fatigue is the same and recovers at the same rate
Compound free weights create high systemic fatigue; isolations create mostly local fatigue
Only mental fatigue matters — physical fatigue is a myth
Isolation exercises are always more fatiguing than compounds

questions.question: An advanced trainee's mesocycle should typically:
questions.explanation: Start at MEV (~10 sets/muscle/week), progressively add 1-2 sets/week to approach MRV by week 4-5, then deload (reduce to ~50% volume). This accumulation-deload pattern manages fatigue while maximizing productive stimulus across the block.

options.text: Maintain the same volume every week for 12+ weeks
Start at maximum volume and taper down
Progressively increase volume from MEV toward MRV, then deload to dissipate fatigue
Randomly vary volume each week with no structure
